name: Vicarious
summary: Ambitious visual-cortex-inspired AI/robotics-vision startup backed by Musk, Bezos, and Thiel promised human-like visual perception for robots but never shipped a broad commercial product; Alphabet's Intrinsic acquired most of its team and IP in 2022 in what was widely seen as a distressed wind-down.
lesson: A decade of prestige funding and brand-name backers cannot substitute for a clear path from research breakthroughs to a shippable commercial product.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Alphabet Intrinsic's acquisition of Vicarious, 2022

name: Matroid
summary: Stanford spinout led by Reza Zadeh built a no-code platform for training custom object-detection models but ceased independent operations around 2023 as larger cloud-vendor AI platforms commoditized the no-code computer-vision tooling category.
lesson: No-code AI tooling layered on top of commodity object-detection models is vulnerable once cloud giants bundle the same capability for free.
source: Industry trade press and Crunchbase status update on Matroid, 2023

name: Orbital Insight
summary: Satellite and geospatial imagery analytics pioneer that used computer vision to track parking lots, oil storage, and supply chains raised over $128M from top-tier VCs but ceased operations in February 2024 after failing to find a sustainable path to profitability.
lesson: Novel alternative-data insights from satellite imagery can impress hedge-fund pilots without ever building a large enough recurring enterprise customer base to cover the cost of imagery and analysis.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Orbital Insight's shutdown, February 2024

name: Argo AI
summary: Backed by Ford and Volkswagen with over $3.6B invested, Argo AI built one of the most advanced autonomous-vehicle perception and self-driving stacks in the industry but was shut down in October 2022 after its automaker backers concluded full self-driving was too far off to justify continued funding.
lesson: Even with billions in strategic-investor backing, a perception stack aimed at full autonomy can be abandoned the moment corporate parents' patience for a distant commercialization horizon runs out.
source: Reuters and TechCrunch coverage of the Argo AI shutdown, October 2022

name: Kairos
summary: Miami-based facial-recognition API startup, whose founder publicly refused to sell the technology to law enforcement over civil-rights concerns, struggled commercially and effectively ceased operations around 2020 after years of executive turnover and thin revenue.
lesson: Taking a principled stand against a lucrative but ethically fraught customer segment can be right for society while still starving a startup of the revenue it needs to survive.
source: TechCrunch and Miami Herald coverage of Kairos's struggles and wind-down

name: AnyVision (Oosto)
summary: Israeli facial-recognition company lost a planned $74M investment from Microsoft's M12 fund in 2020 after an internal audit found its technology had allegedly been used for mass surveillance of Palestinians in the West Bank; it rebranded to Oosto in 2022 amid continued reputational and commercial distress.
lesson: Facial-recognition companies that cannot control or verify how governments and security customers deploy their technology risk losing marquee investors overnight.
source: NBC News and TechCrunch coverage of Microsoft's withdrawal from AnyVision, 2020

name: Umbo Computer Vision
summary: Taiwan-based AI security-camera startup that used computer vision to detect anomalous activity in real time raised funding from Y Combinator and others but wound down commercial operations by around 2021 amid intense competition in the smart-security-camera category.
lesson: AI-powered security cameras compete against both well-funded startups and giant incumbents simultaneously, leaving little room for a mid-funded independent player.
source: Crunchbase and Taiwan tech press coverage of Umbo CV's decline

name: PointGrab
summary: Israeli computer-vision company built low-resolution optical sensors for smart-building occupancy detection and gesture control, but after pivoting away from consumer gesture control to building analytics, it wound down operations around 2019 having failed to reach scale in either market.
lesson: Pivoting a vision sensor platform between consumer gesture control and enterprise building analytics can leave a company without deep expertise or a defensible position in either market.
source: Israeli tech press (Calcalist/Globes) coverage of PointGrab's wind-down

name: Kuna Systems
summary: Smart home security-light and camera startup using onboard computer vision for motion and person detection ceased operations in mid-2019, leaving customers' cloud-connected devices with degraded functionality.
lesson: Consumer smart-home hardware with cloud-dependent computer vision features leaves customers stranded the moment the company runs out of runway.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Kuna Systems' shutdown, 2019

name: CloudSight (CamFind)
summary: Maker of the CamFind visual-search app, which let users photograph any object to search the web for it, wound down its consumer app and pivoted its underlying CloudSight image-recognition API business before effectively fading out by around 2018 as larger platforms folded similar visual search into their own apps.
lesson: A clever visual-search demo can attract early app-store buzz, but it is defenseless once Google, Amazon, and Pinterest bundle the same capability natively.
source: Crunchbase and tech press coverage of CloudSight/CamFind's decline

name: Slyce
summary: Publicly traded visual-search and image-recognition company that partnered with retailers for 'snap-to-shop' commerce features saw its stock collapse and effectively wound down commercial operations around 2019 after failing to convert retail pilots into durable recurring revenue.
lesson: Retail visual-search pilots can generate press coverage without ever becoming a line item retailers are willing to pay for at scale.
source: Canadian small-cap market coverage of Slyce Inc.'s decline, 2018-2019

name: Zeekit
summary: Israeli virtual try-on startup used computer vision and body modeling to let shoppers see clothing on their own photos; Walmart acquired it in 2021 for a reported ~$200M-$300M but shut the unit down entirely in early 2023 as part of a broader retreat from acquired tech units.
lesson: A high-profile strategic acquisition is not a guarantee of survival if the acquirer's own priorities shift within a couple of years.
source: Bloomberg and TechCrunch coverage of Walmart's shutdown of Zeekit, 2023

name: Perceptive Automata
summary: MIT-spinout building computer-vision models that predicted pedestrian and cyclist intent for autonomous-vehicle perception stacks, partnering with Toyota, Hyundai, and Daimler, but ceased operations in 2023 as AV-industry funding and demand contracted sharply.
lesson: A narrow, technically excellent perception niche can still be swept away when the entire downstream autonomous-vehicle industry pulls back on spending.
source: Trade press coverage of Perceptive Automata's shutdown, 2023

name: Nauto
summary: AI dashcam company using computer vision to detect driver distraction and predict collisions for commercial fleets raised over $170M from investors including SoftBank and BMW, but underwent major layoffs and a significant strategic retrenchment starting in 2022-2023 amid weak enterprise conversion.
lesson: Fleet-safety computer vision requires a long enterprise sales cycle and heavy hardware subsidization that can outpace even blue-chip strategic investment.
source: Trade press coverage of Nauto's layoffs and restructuring, 2022-2023

name: AEye
summary: Lidar and perception-software company for autonomous vehicles went public via SPAC in 2021 at a roughly $2B valuation, but its stock collapsed over 95% amid mounting losses and stalled automotive design wins, leaving the company in severe financial distress.
lesson: A SPAC listing at a lofty valuation can accelerate the exposure of weak unit economics in a capital-intensive perception-hardware business.
source: Public SEC filings and market coverage of AEye's stock decline

name: Rekor Systems
summary: License-plate-recognition and roadway computer-vision company has posted years of significant net losses and has repeatedly diluted shareholders through equity raises, drawing going-concern-level scrutiny despite steady government and transportation-agency contracts.
lesson: A steady stream of government contracts does not by itself resolve a computer-vision company's underlying cost structure and path to profitability.
source: Rekor Systems SEC filings and small-cap market coverage

name: CloudMinds
summary: Chinese-American 'cloud robot' and computer-vision AI company backed by SoftBank raised hundreds of millions and was reportedly preparing a large US IPO, but was placed on the US Commerce Department's Entity List in 2020 over national-security concerns and filed for bankruptcy in the US in 2021.
lesson: Cross-border AI companies straddling US and Chinese capital and technology can be existentially exposed to geopolitical export-control decisions outside their control.
source: Reuters coverage of CloudMinds' US Entity List designation and bankruptcy, 2021

name: Standard Cognition (Standard AI)
summary: Autonomous-checkout computer-vision startup that competed directly with Amazon's Just Walk Out raised over $200M but sold off its cashier-less checkout technology and pivoted the company toward AI-powered loss-prevention and inventory tools in 2023 after failing to scale profitable retail deployments.
lesson: Camera-based autonomous checkout requires enormous per-store hardware and integration cost that few retailers were ultimately willing to pay for at scale.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Standard AI's pivot away from autonomous checkout, 2023

name: Descartes Labs
summary: Los Alamos-spinout satellite-imagery and geospatial AI analytics company laid off roughly 40% of its staff in 2020 and underwent repeated strategic pivots across agriculture, government, and defense verticals as it struggled to convert its imagery platform into durable enterprise revenue.
lesson: A technically impressive geospatial data platform can still struggle commercially if it never settles on one vertical willing to pay recurring subscription prices.
source: Trade press coverage of Descartes Labs' 2020 layoffs and subsequent pivots

name: Megvii (Face++)
summary: One of China's 'AI Dragon' facial-recognition giants was added to the US Entity List in 2019 over alleged human-rights abuses in Xinjiang, which crippled its international expansion and forced it to repeatedly withdraw and refile IPO applications in Hong Kong over several years amid mounting losses.
lesson: Facial-recognition technology sold into state-surveillance programs can permanently close off international capital markets and customers even for a technically dominant player.
source: Reuters coverage of Megvii's US blacklisting and stalled Hong Kong IPO

name: SenseTime
summary: Once the world's most valuable AI startup and a leading computer-vision unicorn, SenseTime's Hong Kong-listed stock lost roughly 90% of its value in the years after its 2021 IPO amid US sanctions, Xinjiang-related human-rights allegations, and years of mounting net losses.
lesson: Enormous pre-IPO valuations built on government-surveillance contracts can evaporate once sanctions and human-rights scrutiny cut off both customers and capital.
source: Reuters and Bloomberg coverage of SenseTime's post-IPO stock collapse and US sanctions

name: BlackSky
summary: Satellite-imagery and AI-analytics company went public via SPAC in 2021 but has posted persistent net losses and a steep stock decline from its debut valuation, reflecting the difficulty of turning frequent satellite revisits and computer-vision analytics into durable profitability.
lesson: High-cadence satellite imagery is expensive to produce and analyze, and demand from government and commercial customers has not yet caught up to the capital required to sustain it.
source: BlackSky SEC filings and public market coverage of its stock performance

name: Clearview AI
summary: Facial-recognition company that scraped billions of images from the public web to build a law-enforcement search tool has faced GDPR fines exceeding tens of millions of euros, an outright ban on new customers in Illinois under a 2022 BIPA settlement, and prohibitions or fines in Canada, Australia, the UK, France, Italy, and Greece.
lesson: Building a facial-recognition database from scraped personal data without consent can generate enough regulatory liability across jurisdictions to permanently constrain a company's addressable market.
source: Reuters and ICO/CNIL regulatory action coverage of Clearview AI, 2020-2023

name: Drive.ai
summary: Stanford-AI-Lab spinout building a computer-vision-centric approach to autonomous-vehicle perception ran low on cash after failing to close a new funding round, and was acquired by Apple in mid-2019 in what amounted to a distressed, below-valuation talent acquisition.
lesson: Autonomous-vehicle perception is one of the most capital-intensive bets in computer vision, and a single failed funding round can force a fire-sale exit.
source: Axios and TechCrunch coverage of Apple's acquisition of Drive.ai, 2019

name: AImotive
summary: Hungarian autonomous-vehicle perception and simulation software company raised significant funding from investors including Cisco and Samsung but underwent substantial layoffs and downsizing by 2023 as automaker demand for third-party full-stack AV perception software failed to materialize at scale.
lesson: Selling a full autonomous-driving perception stack to automakers is a multi-decade capital commitment that can outlast a venture-funded startup's patience.
source: European tech press coverage of AImotive's downsizing, 2023

name: ZenRobotics
summary: Finnish pioneer of computer-vision-guided robotic waste-sorting systems went through severe financial distress and restructuring around 2018-2019 before being acquired out of distress by Terex, ending its run as an independent company.
lesson: Industrial computer-vision robotics requires long sales cycles and heavy capex that can exhaust even a first-mover's funding before the market matures.
source: Finnish and European industrial-tech press coverage of ZenRobotics' restructuring and Terex acquisition

name: Blippar
summary: UK-based augmented-reality and image-recognition 'visual browser' startup, once valued at over $1.5B, collapsed into insolvency administration in November 2018 after burning through its funding without finding a sustainable revenue model, and was later acquired for a fraction of its peak valuation.
lesson: A large valuation built on a flashy image-recognition demo can mask years of unprofitable operations until the funding simply runs out.
source: Financial Times and TechCrunch coverage of Blippar's 2018 administration filing

name: ObjectVideo
summary: Early pioneer of video-analytics computer vision for security and surveillance built a large patent portfolio and pivoted increasingly toward patent licensing and litigation before winding down as an operating product company by around 2017 amid legal disputes and restructuring.
lesson: A strong early patent position in computer vision is not a substitute for a durable operating product business as the underlying technology commoditizes.
source: Trade press coverage of ObjectVideo's patent-licensing pivot and eventual wind-down

name: FaceFirst
summary: Retail-security facial-recognition vendor faced a wave of Illinois BIPA class-action lawsuits and reputational damage after a major retail customer's use of its technology drew media scrutiny, contributing to a sharp contraction in its retail customer base through the early 2020s.
lesson: Selling facial recognition into consumer-facing retail environments carries acute biometric-privacy litigation risk that can outweigh the revenue from any single large account.
source: Trade and legal press coverage of BIPA litigation involving FaceFirst retail deployments

name: Deep Vision Inc.
summary: Edge AI chipmaker building low-power computer-vision processors for cameras and IoT devices raised significant venture funding but was quietly absorbed by an acquirer for a fraction of its raise around 2023 after failing to win enough design wins against larger edge-AI silicon competitors.
lesson: Custom silicon for edge computer vision requires winning design sockets years in advance of revenue, a race that well-capitalized incumbents are better positioned to win.
source: Semiconductor trade press coverage of Deep Vision's funding and eventual acquisition

name: EarthNow
funding: undisclosed (backed by Bill Gates, Airbus, SoftBank)
summary: Ambitious satellite venture backed by Bill Gates and Airbus promised a constellation delivering real-time video of the entire planet analyzed with computer vision, but the project stalled and quietly wound down around 2021 without ever launching an operational satellite.
lesson: Marquee investor names cannot compress the enormous capital and engineering timelines required to build and launch a real-time global imaging constellation.
source: SpaceNews coverage of EarthNow's stalled satellite constellation plans

name: UrtheCast
summary: Canadian satellite-imagery and geospatial-analytics company that operated cameras on the International Space Station went through years of mounting losses, was delisted from the Toronto Stock Exchange, and ceased satellite operations around 2020-2021 after failing to find a buyer for the whole business.
lesson: Publicly traded satellite-imagery companies can run out of runway well before their underlying computer-vision analytics business reaches profitable scale.
source: Canadian securities-market coverage of UrtheCast's delisting and wind-down

name: Neurala
summary: Deep-learning computer-vision company that pivoted from consumer robotics and drone vision toward manufacturing visual-inspection software underwent significant downsizing through the early 2020s as it struggled to reach scale against larger industrial-AI competitors.
lesson: Repeated pivots across consumer robotics, drones, and industrial inspection can leave a computer-vision company without a defensible position in any one market.
source: Industry trade press coverage of Neurala's shift to manufacturing inspection and subsequent downsizing

name: Trigo
summary: Israeli autonomous-checkout computer-vision company that partnered with major grocery chains including Tesco and Aldi Süd raised over $200M but conducted significant layoffs in 2023 as the wider cashier-less checkout category struggled with high per-store camera and compute costs.
lesson: Even well-funded, blue-chip-partnered autonomous-checkout vendors face a category-wide unit-economics problem that no single company has yet solved.
source: Calcalist and CTech coverage of Trigo's 2023 layoffs

name: Clarifai
summary: Founded by ImageNet-competition winner Matthew Zeiler, Clarifai built a widely used image and video recognition API platform, later expanding into generative AI and full-stack AI orchestration while remaining an independent, well-funded leader in commercial computer vision.
lesson: Winning technical credibility in a foundational benchmark can translate into a durable developer-platform business if the API stays ahead of the commoditization curve.
source: Crunchbase funding history and industry coverage of Clarifai

name: Hive
summary: Hive built a suite of computer-vision and machine-learning APIs for content moderation, deepfake detection, and media understanding, becoming a key vendor for social platforms needing to detect harmful visual content at scale, and reached profitability while continuing to expand into generative AI.
lesson: Selling computer vision as an API for a painful, recurring enterprise need like content moderation can build a durable, capital-efficient business.
source: Crunchbase funding history and trade press coverage of Hive AI

name: Cortica
summary: Israeli company built an unsupervised, brain-inspired approach to computer vision for autonomous vehicles and smart cities, partnering with automakers including Nissan's alliance and Chinese OEMs to license its perception technology for production vehicles.
lesson: A differentiated, patent-protected approach to visual perception can win automotive licensing deals even against much larger AV-perception competitors.
source: Trade press coverage of Cortica's automotive partnerships and funding rounds

name: Kneron
summary: Edge AI chip company building low-power neural-processing units optimized for on-device computer vision (facial recognition, object detection) raised a large Series C in 2023 backed by Foxconn and Qualcomm-affiliated investors and continued shipping chips into smartphones, cars, and IoT devices.
lesson: Purpose-built silicon for on-device computer vision can attract strategic semiconductor and manufacturing investors even in a capital-intensive chip market.
source: Trade press coverage of Kneron's 2023 Series C funding round

name: Mobileye
funding: acquired by Intel for $15.3B (2017); IPO'd Nasdaq 2022
summary: Pioneer of camera-based ADAS and autonomous-driving perception chips became the dominant supplier of computer-vision-based driver-assistance systems to global automakers, was acquired by Intel for $15.3B in 2017, and went public again in a 2022 IPO that valued it at over $16B.
lesson: Becoming the default camera-based perception supplier across dozens of automakers over two decades builds an extraordinarily durable moat.
source: Intel and Mobileye press releases; Reuters coverage of the 2022 Mobileye IPO

name: Netradyne
summary: AI dashcam company using computer vision to score commercial driver behavior in real time scaled to become a major fleet-safety vendor, reaching a reported valuation above $1.35B by 2023 and continuing to add large trucking and delivery-fleet customers.
lesson: Pairing computer vision with a clear ROI metric fleets already track, like accident reduction, can drive fast enterprise adoption.
source: Trade press coverage of Netradyne's funding rounds and valuation

name: Viz.ai
summary: Medical-imaging computer-vision company that automatically flags stroke indicators on CT scans and alerts specialists became the first AI software reimbursed by US Medicare (2020) and scaled to hundreds of hospital systems, reaching unicorn status.
lesson: Securing a reimbursement pathway alongside strong clinical evidence can turn a diagnostic computer-vision tool into a scalable, revenue-generating hospital product.
source: Trade press coverage of Viz.ai's CMS reimbursement approval and funding rounds

name: Aidoc
summary: Israeli medical-imaging AI company builds FDA-cleared computer-vision algorithms that flag critical findings (strokes, pulmonary embolisms, fractures) on radiology scans, and scaled to deployment across more than a thousand hospitals worldwide.
lesson: Stacking multiple FDA clearances across different clinical use cases lets a single medical-imaging computer-vision platform expand deployment within the same hospital systems.
source: Trade press coverage of Aidoc's FDA clearances and funding rounds

name: Landing AI
summary: Founded by AI pioneer Andrew Ng, Landing AI built LandingLens, a computer-vision platform for manufacturing visual inspection that lets factories train defect-detection models with small labeled datasets, scaling to enterprise manufacturing customers globally.
lesson: Reducing the labeled-data requirement for industrial visual inspection removes the biggest adoption barrier for manufacturers new to computer vision.
source: Trade press coverage of Landing AI's funding and enterprise deployments

name: Trax
summary: Retail-shelf computer-vision company that uses smartphone and robot-captured images to automatically track product placement and out-of-stocks became a unicorn serving major CPG brands and retailers worldwide, backed by SoftBank and other large investors.
lesson: Solving a chronic, expensive retail-execution problem like shelf out-of-stocks with computer vision can build a globally scalable, high-margin SaaS business.
source: Crunchbase funding history and trade press coverage of Trax's growth

name: Cape Analytics
summary: Uses computer vision on aerial and satellite imagery to automatically assess property risk characteristics (roof condition, vegetation, pools) for the insurance industry, scaling to become a standard underwriting data source for major US property insurers.
lesson: Targeting a single well-defined, high-value data need for one industry (insurance underwriting) can make aerial computer vision immediately monetizable.
source: Trade press coverage of Cape Analytics' insurance-industry partnerships and funding

name: ViSenze
summary: Singapore-based visual-search and visual-commerce computer-vision company powers 'shop the look' and similar-item search features for major e-commerce platforms across Asia, including partnerships with Rakuten and other large retailers.
lesson: Embedding visual search directly into e-commerce platforms' existing shopping flows turns a computer-vision feature into a measurable conversion-rate lift retailers will pay for.
source: Trade press coverage of ViSenze's e-commerce partnerships and funding rounds

name: Syte
summary: Formerly Fashwell, this Israeli visual AI company powers image-based product discovery and 'shop similar' recommendations for fashion and home-goods e-commerce retailers, scaling across hundreds of online retail customers globally.
lesson: Focusing visual search on a single vertical like fashion, where visual similarity strongly predicts purchase intent, sharpens product-market fit versus general-purpose visual search.
source: Trade press coverage of Syte's funding rounds and retail partnerships

name: Mighty AI
summary: Data-annotation and training-data platform specializing in labeling imagery for autonomous-vehicle perception systems was acquired by Uber's Advanced Technologies Group in 2019 to strengthen its self-driving perception data pipeline.
lesson: Rather than building a perception stack itself, a company can build durable value as critical infrastructure supplying the labeled data every perception stack needs.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Uber ATG's acquisition of Mighty AI, 2019

name: AI.Reverie
summary: Synthetic-data startup that generated photorealistic simulated imagery to train computer-vision models for defense, retail, and autonomous-vehicle applications was acquired by Meta in 2021 to bolster its AI training-data infrastructure.
lesson: Synthetic training data addresses one of computer vision's most persistent bottlenecks (labeled real-world imagery), making it a strategically attractive acquisition target for large AI labs.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Meta's acquisition of AI.Reverie, 2021

name: Datagen
summary: Israeli synthetic-data platform generates simulated humans and environments to train computer-vision models for robotics, AR/VR, and automotive applications, scaling revenue by selling to major tech and automotive customers needing training data at a scale real-world capture cannot match.
lesson: Synthetic data generation sidesteps the privacy and labeling-cost bottlenecks that constrain traditional computer-vision training pipelines.
source: Trade press coverage of Datagen's funding rounds and enterprise customers

name: Vigilant Solutions
summary: License-plate-recognition computer-vision company built one of the largest commercial LPR data networks in the US for law enforcement and repossession industries, and was acquired by Motorola Solutions in 2019 to expand its public-safety software portfolio.
lesson: Building a large, defensible proprietary data network around a narrow computer-vision use case can make a company a natural roll-up target for public-safety incumbents.
source: Motorola Solutions press release on the Vigilant Solutions acquisition, 2019

name: Dropcam
summary: Consumer smart-camera company that used cloud-based computer vision for motion and activity detection was acquired by Google (via Nest) for $555M in 2014, becoming the foundation of Nest's video-camera product line.
lesson: Being an early, well-executed mover in consumer home security video can make a company a prime acquisition target for platform players entering the smart-home category.
source: Google/Nest press release and TechCrunch coverage of the Dropcam acquisition, 2014

name: Zebra Medical Vision
summary: Israeli medical-imaging AI company built a broad portfolio of FDA-cleared computer-vision algorithms for radiology, and was acquired by medical-imaging device maker Nanox in 2021 to combine its AI analytics with Nanox's imaging hardware.
lesson: Accumulating a wide portfolio of regulatory-cleared diagnostic algorithms builds a strategically valuable asset for hardware makers seeking an AI layer.
source: Trade press coverage of Nanox's acquisition of Zebra Medical Vision, 2021

name: Mapillary
summary: Swedish street-level imagery platform used computer vision to automatically extract map features (signs, lanes, addresses) from crowdsourced photos, and was acquired by Facebook (Meta) in 2020 to strengthen its mapping and AR data infrastructure.
lesson: Crowdsourced imagery paired with automated computer-vision feature extraction can build a mapping data asset valuable enough for a platform giant to acquire outright.
source: Meta press release and TechCrunch coverage of the Mapillary acquisition, 2020

name: Onfido
summary: Identity-verification company used computer vision and facial biometrics to match selfies against government ID documents at scale, becoming a leading KYC vendor for banks and fintechs before being acquired by Entrust in 2024 in a deal reported near $650M.
lesson: Regulatory-driven KYC demand from banks and fintechs can turn a facial-biometric verification product into a durable, exit-ready enterprise business.
source: Entrust press release and trade press coverage of the Onfido acquisition, 2024

name: Trueface
summary: US-based facial-recognition and weapon-detection computer-vision company focused on physical-security and access-control use cases was acquired by government-services contractor Serco in 2022 to expand its public-sector security-technology offerings.
lesson: Focusing facial-recognition technology narrowly on access control and threat detection, rather than broad surveillance, can avoid the regulatory backlash that sank competitors in the same space.
source: Trade press coverage of Serco's acquisition of Trueface, 2022

name: EyeVerify
summary: Developer of 'Eyeprint' eye-vein biometric authentication using smartphone cameras was acquired by Ant Financial (Alibaba affiliate) in 2016 to add biometric authentication to Alipay and other Ant Financial products.
lesson: A defensible, hard-to-spoof biometric computer-vision technique can be acquired by a payments giant looking to strengthen authentication at massive scale.
source: Trade press coverage of Ant Financial's acquisition of EyeVerify, 2016

name: DeepScale
summary: Berkeley-spinout building efficient deep-learning computer-vision perception software for low-power automotive chips was acquired by Tesla in 2019 to strengthen its in-house Autopilot perception team.
lesson: Specialized expertise in squeezing accurate perception models onto low-power automotive hardware can be a decisive acquisition target for an OEM building its own self-driving stack.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Tesla's acquisition of DeepScale, 2019

name: Zoox
summary: Built a purpose-designed autonomous robotaxi with an end-to-end computer-vision and sensor-fusion perception stack, and after a period of financial distress was acquired by Amazon in 2020 for roughly $1.2B, continuing to test and expand robotaxi service through the mid-2020s.
lesson: A near-death funding crisis in a capital-intensive perception business can still end in a favorable strategic acquisition if the underlying technology remains differentiated.
source: Amazon press release and Reuters coverage of the Zoox acquisition, 2020

name: Metaio
summary: German pioneer of markerless computer-vision-based augmented reality and object tracking, whose SDK underpinned many early AR try-on and industrial AR applications, was acquired by Apple in 2015 and its technology was folded into Apple's ARKit.
lesson: Foundational computer-vision tracking technology can become the acquired backbone of a much larger platform's AR strategy years before that platform becomes public.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Apple's acquisition of Metaio, 2015

name: Digital Diagnostics (IDx)
summary: Developer of IDx-DR, the first FDA-authorized fully autonomous AI diagnostic system, uses computer vision to screen retinal images for diabetic retinopathy without a specialist reviewing the image, and scaled deployment across primary-care clinics in the US.
lesson: Achieving the first-ever FDA authorization for a fully autonomous diagnostic computer-vision system created a durable regulatory and reputational moat in medical AI.
source: FDA press release on IDx-DR authorization, 2018, and subsequent trade press coverage

name: Movidius
summary: Irish maker of low-power vision-processing chips designed specifically for on-device computer vision (used in DJI drones, Google Clips, and other embedded vision products) was acquired by Intel in 2016 to anchor Intel's edge-AI and computer-vision silicon strategy.
lesson: Purpose-built vision-processing silicon that already powers multiple third-party hardware products becomes an attractive acquisition for chipmakers entering edge AI.
source: Intel press release and trade press coverage of the Movidius acquisition, 2016
